question: [File: 'File Name: graphql/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/graphql/config/GraphQlConfiguration.java -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: graphQlCustomizer()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the PreparsedDocumentProvider cache's expireAfterWrite=1h TTL by submitting a query that caches a document with a specific transaction filter, then waiting for the cache to serve stale transaction data to other users after the underlying transaction state has changed?

[File: 'File Name: graphql/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/graphql/config/GraphQlConfiguration.java -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: graphQlCustomizer()] Can an unprivileged external user craft a query where the MaxQueryComplexityInstrumentation(200) calculation underestimates the actual resolver cost for transaction fields (e.g., by using fragments that expand to many fields), causing the server to execute a query with effective complexity far exceeding 200 and returning excessive transaction data?

[File: 'File Name: graphql/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/graphql/config/GraphQlConfiguration.java -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: graphQlCustomizer()] Can an unprivileged external user use GraphQL inline fragments on transaction types to bypass MaxQueryDepthInstrumentation(10) depth counting, since inline fragments may not increment the depth counter, allowing effectively deeper queries that expose nested transaction data beyond the intended limit?

[File: 'File Name: graphql/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/graphql/config/GraphQlConfiguration.java -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: validationDirectives()] Can an unprivileged external user submit a transaction query where a @constraint directive on a transaction amount field fails with RETURN_NULL, causing the amount field to be silently nulled out in the response, making a transaction appear to have a zero or null amount when it actually has a non-zero value, misleading the caller about transaction state?

[File: 'File Name: graphql/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/graphql/config/GraphQlConfiguration.java -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: validationDirectives()] Can an unprivileged external user craft a transaction query input that intentionally triggers RETURN_NULL on a non-null transaction field, causing the GraphQL response to omit that field entirely, and then use the absence of the field to infer information about the transaction's internal state?

[File: 'File Name: graphql/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/graphql/config/GraphQlConfiguration.java -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: runtimeWiringConfigurer()] Can an unprivileged external user supply a GraphQlTimestamp value with Unicode normalization characters embedded in the ISO 8601 string that Java's Instant.parse() handles differently than the GraphQL parser, causing a timestamp mismatch that queries transactions from a different time window than intended?

[File: 'File Name: graphql/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/graphql/config/GraphQlConfiguration.java -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: runtimeWiringConfigurer()] Can an unprivileged external user pass a GraphQlDuration value of PT0S (zero duration) as a transaction validity window filter, causing the resolver to return transactions with a zero-length validity window that may represent a special or unintended transaction state?

[File: 'File Name: graphql/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/graphql/config/GraphQlConfiguration.java -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: jacksonCustomizer()] Can an unprivileged external user send a JSON request body with maxDocumentLength(11000) bytes where the extra bytes are injected into a transaction hash field, causing the hash to be silently truncated or corrupted, leading the server to look up a different transaction than the one the attacker specified?

[File: 'File Name: graphql/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/graphql/config/GraphQlConfiguration.java -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: jacksonCustomizer()] Can an unprivileged external user craft a JSON body where the 'query' field string is exactly maxStringLength(11000) characters and contains a transaction query with embedded null bytes or control characters that are accepted by Jackson but cause the GraphQL parser to interpret the query differently, bypassing parser-level limits?

[File: 'File Name: graphql/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/graphql/config/GraphQlConfiguration.java -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: static{}] Can an unprivileged external user send a GraphQL query that uses Unicode escape sequences within transaction field string literals to stay under maxCharacters(10000) while encoding a longer effective string value, bypassing the character limit and injecting oversized transaction filter values?

[File: 'File Name: graphql/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/graphql/config/GraphQlConfiguration.java -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: graphQlCustomizer()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that PreparsedDocumentProvider caches by raw query string (not by variables) to submit the same query string with different variable values, where the cached parsed document is reused but the variables are not re-validated, allowing tampered variable values to bypass validation on subsequent requests?

[File: 'File Name: graphql/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/graphql/config/GraphQlConfiguration.java -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: graphQlCustomizer()] Can an unprivileged external user submit a transaction query with a variable whose value is a crafted Long scalar at Long.MAX_VALUE (9223372036854775807, 19 digits matching maxNumberLength(19)), causing arithmetic overflow in transaction ID resolution that maps to a negative or zero entity ID?

[File: 'File Name: graphql/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/graphql/config/GraphQlConfiguration.java -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: runtimeWiringConfigurer()] Can an unprivileged external user supply a GraphQlTimestamp scalar value of '1970-01-01T00:00:00Z' (epoch zero) as a transaction timestamp filter, causing the resolver to return all transactions from the beginning of the ledger history, effectively dumping the entire transaction history?

[File: 'File Name: graphql/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/graphql/config/GraphQlConfiguration.java -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: runtimeWiringConfigurer()] Can an unprivileged external user supply a GraphQlTimestamp scalar with a future timestamp far beyond the current consensus time, causing the transaction query to return an empty result set that misleads the caller into believing a submitted transaction does not exist when it actually does?

[File: 'File Name: graphql/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/graphql/config/GraphQlConfiguration.java -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: validationDirectives()] Can an unprivileged external user submit a transaction query where multiple validation directives on the same transaction input field all fail with RETURN_NULL, causing the field to be silently dropped from the query execution context, and the resolver to use a default or null value that matches a different transaction?

[File: 'File Name: graphql/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/graphql/config/GraphQlConfiguration.java -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: graphQlCustomizer()] Can an unprivileged external user send concurrent requests with the same query string to the PreparsedDocumentProvider AsyncCache, exploiting a race condition in the CompletableFuture-based cache population to cause two different parsed documents to be associated with the same cache key, leading to non-deterministic transaction query results?

[File: 'File Name: graphql/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/graphql/config/GraphQlConfiguration.java -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: jacksonCustomizer()] Can an unprivileged external user craft a JSON request where the 'variables' object has exactly maxTokenCount(100) tokens, all targeting transaction input fields, causing the Jackson parser to accept the maximum number of variable tokens while the GraphQL engine processes them all, maximizing the attack surface for variable-based transaction tampering?

[File: 'File Name: graphql/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/graphql/config/GraphQlConfiguration.java -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: static{}] Can an unprivileged external user craft a GraphQL query that uses exactly maxTokens(1000) tokens to define a transaction query with many aliased fields, where each alias maps to the same underlying transaction field, causing the server to return the same transaction data field 1000 times in a single response, amplifying data exfiltration?

[File: 'File Name: graphql/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/graphql/config/GraphQlConfiguration.java -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: runtimeWiringConfigurer()] Can an unprivileged external user supply an ExtendedScalars.Object value containing a key named '__proto__' or 'constructor' in a transaction input, exploiting prototype pollution-like behavior in the Java object deserialization path to tamper with the transaction query execution context?

[File: 'File Name: graphql/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/graphql/config/GraphQlConfiguration.java -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: runtimeWiringConfigurer()] Can an unprivileged external user supply a GraphQlDuration scalar with a value like 'P0D' (zero days) versus 'PT0S' (zero seconds) that are semantically equivalent but may be handled differently by Duration.parse(), causing inconsistent transaction time window calculations?

[File: 'File Name: graphql/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/graphql/config/GraphQlConfiguration.java -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: graphQlCustomizer()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the MaxQueryComplexityInstrumentation(200) by using GraphQL fragments that are referenced multiple times in a transaction query, where each fragment reference adds to the complexity count but the actual resolver execution is deduplicated, allowing the attacker to stay under the complexity limit while maximizing data retrieval?

[File: 'File Name: graphql/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/graphql/config/GraphQlConfiguration.java -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: jacksonCustomizer()] Can an unprivileged external user send a JSON request where the 'query' field value contains a transaction query string of exactly 11000 characters (maxStringLength) that is also exactly 11000 bytes (maxDocumentLength), and the last characters encode a transaction ID that differs from what the attacker claims to be querying, causing a mismatch between the stated and actual transaction lookup?

[File: 'File Name: graphql/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/graphql/config/GraphQlConfiguration.java -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: static{}] Can an unprivileged external user craft a GraphQL query that uses maxRuleDepth(100) nested inline fragments on a transaction type, where each nesting level adds a field selection, causing the validation engine to process 100 levels of fragment nesting before enforcing depth limits, potentially exposing transaction fields that should be inaccessible at lower depths?

[File: 'File Name: graphql/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/graphql/config/GraphQlConfiguration.java -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: validationDirectives()] Can an unprivileged external user submit a transaction query where a @size or @range validation directive on a transaction amount field returns null due to RETURN_NULL strategy, causing the amount to be treated as null in the response and misleading the caller into thinking the transaction has no associated amount?

[File: 'File Name: graphql/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/graphql/config/GraphQlConfiguration.java -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: graphQlCustomizer()] Can an unprivileged external user send a batch of GraphQL requests where each request is a transaction query at the complexity limit of 200, causing the server to process many high-complexity transaction queries simultaneously and exhausting database connection pool resources, denying service to legitimate transaction queries?
